title: The role of the trapezius in carrying emotional burdens
author: Kalesh

The weight you carry without knowing 
 I spent years with a knot in my right trapezius that wouldn't budge. Massage therapists would dig their elbows in a...
htmlContent:
The weight you carry without knowing

I spent years with a knot in my right trapezius that wouldn't budge. Massage therapists would dig their elbows in and say "you're really holding on here" and I'd nod like I understood. But I didn't. Not really. I thought it was just stress from sitting at a computer. Maybe bad posture from years of slouching. I tried foam rolling, stretching, even acupuncture. The knot would soften for a day, maybe two, then return like an old friend who doesn't know when to leave.

Here's the thing. Your trapezius isn't just a muscle that helps you shrug and rotate your shoulders. It's intimately connected to your nervous system in ways that most of us never learn about. And for trauma survivors, this muscle becomes a kind of storage unit for emotional weight we never consented to carry.

Think about it. When you're scared, what do you do? You raise your shoulders toward your ears. It's a startle reflex, a protective response. Your trapezius engages to pull your head down into your shoulders like a turtle retreating into its shell. This is normal, adaptive, life-saving in actual danger. But here's where it gets complicated. If you grew up in an environment where danger was chronic, unpredictable, or inescapable, your trapezius never got the signal to fully relax. It stayed in a state of partial contraction, waiting, always waiting, for the next threat.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist and researcher behind the Trauma Center at Brookline, spent decades documenting how trauma lives in the body. In his book The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), he shows how the body's response to trauma isn't just a psychological memory. It's a physiological reality. Your muscles remember. Your fascia remembers. Your trapezius remembers the exact configuration it held when you were six years old and trying to make yourself small and invisible in a room where adults were shouting.

I'm not being metaphorical here. The trapezius connects to your skull, your cervical spine, your thoracic spine, and your shoulder blades. It's a massive, diamond-shaped muscle that spans the back of your neck and upper back. And it's directly innervated by the accessory nerve, which also connects to your vagus nerve. Stay with me here. The vagus nerve is the main highway of your parasympathetic nervous system, the part that's supposed to calm you down after danger passes. When your vagus nerve is compromised by chronic stress or trauma, your trapezius gets stuck in that protective shrug. It can't let go.

The nervous system's reluctant soldier

Stephen Porges developed polyvagal theory in the 1990s, and it changed how we understand the connection between our nerves and our muscles. His work shows that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger, a process he calls neuroception. And your trapezius is one of the primary muscles that responds to what your nervous system detects.

When your neuroception picks up cues of danger, even subtle ones you're not consciously aware of, your trapezius tenses. It's preparing you to fight, to flee, or to freeze. The problem is that for many of us, especially those with complex trauma histories, our nervous system is calibrated to detect danger everywhere. A tone of voice, a facial expression, a sudden movement. Your trapezius responds before your conscious brain even registers what's happening.

I remember sitting in a coffee shop once, and a man behind me dropped a metal tray. Every single person in the room jumped, but I was the one whose shoulders stayed up around my ears for the next twenty minutes. That's not normal. That's a nervous system that never learned how to come back down. That's a trapezius that's been doing overtime for decades.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) by Deb Dana. She breaks down how we can start to recognize these patterns and gently work with our nervous system instead of against it. I found it helpful because it doesn't just explain the science. It gives you ways to actually feel what's happening in your body and begin to shift it.

Your trapezius also holds emotional burdens in a more literal sense. Think about what it means to carry something. You load weight onto your shoulders. You brace your upper back. You engage your trapezius to stabilize that load. Now think about emotional burdens. Worry about a loved one. Financial stress. The weight of unresolved grief. The shame of something that happened to you that you never told anyone about. Where does that weight go? It goes into your shoulders. It goes into your trapezius.

I've worked with clients who described their upper back pain as feeling like they were "carrying the world." They weren't being dramatic. They were describing a literal physiological experience. Their trapezius was in a state of chronic contraction, holding a load that never got set down. And here's the cruel irony. The more your trapezius holds this tension, the more your nervous system interprets that tension as a sign of ongoing danger. It becomes a feedback loop. Your body is telling your brain "we're still in danger, look, we're still braced." And your brain keeps your body braced because it believes danger is present.

What it means to let go

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine. He's the founder of Somatic Experiencing, and he writes about how trauma gets trapped in the body when our natural defensive responses are interrupted. A deer that gets chased by a predator will shake and tremble after escaping, releasing the survival energy. We don't do that. We hold it. And that holding happens, in large part, in the muscles of the upper back and neck.

Let me be direct with you. I'm not going to tell you that stretching your trapezius will heal your trauma. That's the kind of oversimplification that makes the wellness industry so frustrating. It's not that simple. Your trapezius is holding tension for a reason. That tension is a communication from your nervous system. It's saying "I don't feel safe enough to let go." And you can't bully your nervous system into feeling safe. You can't stretch your way out of a trauma response. But you can start to listen to what that tension is telling you.

Here's what I've found helpful. Instead of trying to force my trapezius to relax, I started paying attention to it. I'd notice when my shoulders crept up. I'd notice the quality of that tension. Was it a hard, rigid clench or a more diffuse, aching holding? Was it on one side more than the other? Right? And I'd ask myself, without judgment, what just happened. What cue did my nervous system just pick up that made me brace?

This isn't about fixing. It's about befriending. It's about building a relationship with a part of your body that's been doing its best to protect you, even if that protection is no longer needed. Your trapezius isn't your enemy. It's a loyal soldier that never got the message that the war is over.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is the TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). I use it gently, not aggressively. Not to force the muscle to release, but to bring awareness to the area. To let my body know "I see you. I'm here. You don't have to hold this alone right now." It sounds strange, I know. But the body responds to presence more than it responds to force.

Sit with that for a moment. Your trapezius has been holding emotional weight that might not even belong to you. Some of it might be inherited from your family, from the way your parents held tension and passed it down through touch and tone and presence. Some of it might be from experiences you don't even remember consciously. But your body remembers. Your trapezius remembers.

The slow work of release

I'm not going to give you a five-step plan to release your trapezius tension. That would be dishonest. The release happens slowly, non-linearly, in fits and starts. Some days you'll feel your shoulders drop and it'll feel like a miracle. Other days you'll wake up with that familiar knot and feel like you've made no progress at all. Both are real. Both are part of the process.

What I can tell you is what's helped me and the people I work with. First, orienting. This is a polyvagal concept. Look around the room you're in. Find something neutral or pleasant to look at. A plant. A painting. The way light falls on a wall. Let your eyes rest there for thirty seconds. Notice if your shoulders drop even a millimeter. They might. They might not. Either way, you're gathering information about your nervous system.

Second, pendulation. This is from Peter Levine's work. You gently move between awareness of the tension and awareness of a resource, something that feels good or safe in your body. Maybe it's your feet on the floor. Maybe it's your breath. Maybe it's the warmth of your hands. You don't try to get rid of the tension. You just let your system know that both experiences can exist. Tension and ease. Protection and safety. Your trapezius can start to learn that it's okay to let go because safety is also present.

Third, and this is the hardest one for me, is grief. Because sometimes that tension isn't just protection. It's grief. It's the weight of all the times you needed to be held and weren't. It's the sorrow of all the moments you had to be strong when you were falling apart inside. Your trapezius might be carrying grief that has no other place to go. And grief needs to be felt to be released. Not analyzed. Not fixed. Just felt.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did your shoulders tighten or soften? Did your breath change? That's your nervous system responding to the possibility of feeling something you've been avoiding. That's okay. You don't have to go there right now. But know that it's there. And know that your body is waiting for you to be ready.

I don't have a neat conclusion for you. I wish I did. I wish I could tell you that if you just do this one thing, your trapezius will release and you'll feel light and free forever. But that's not how it works. The release is ongoing. The weight gets lighter, but it doesn't disappear. And maybe that's okay. Maybe the goal isn't to never carry anything. Maybe the goal is to carry it differently. With awareness. With compassion. With the knowledge that you can set it down when you're ready, and pick it up again if you need to.

Your trapezius is not broken. It's not a problem to be solved. It's a messenger. And if you can learn to listen to what it's saying, you might find that the message isn't just about pain. It's about survival. It's about protection. It's about a part of you that has been working so hard, for so long, to keep you safe. That deserves gratitude, not force. That deserves presence, not pressure. That deserves you, showing up, again and again, with curiosity instead of demand.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's essential work on how trauma lives in the body
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - Peter Levine's classic on Somatic Experiencing and trauma release
  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical guide to working with the nervous system
  • TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link) - A gentle tool for bringing awareness to stored body tension
